source: They Say / I Say, Chapter 6: planting a naysayer
article: This essay argues that Lady Macbeth is the play's true villain, the figure whose ambition sets the murders in motion and whose collapse arrives only after she has already wrecked the kingdom. Across five sections I trace her speeches, her appeals to her husband, and the staging conventions that frame her in shadow.

critiques.text_quote: This essay argues that Lady Macbeth is the play's true villain, the figure whose ambition sets the murders in motion and whose collapse arrives only after she has already wrecked the kingdom.
critiques.issue_label: no naysayer for a contested thesis
critiques.question: Your thesis is a contested reading and you let no opposing voice into the essay. Janet Adelman's Suffocating Mothers reads Lady Macbeth's ambition as a forced masculine performance the play punishes more harshly than her husband's. A reader who took that reading seriously is who you have to convince.
critiques.why_it_matters: Without the strongest opposing reading on the page, the essay never argues against the position it most needs to defeat.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add a paragraph that names the Adelman reading at its strongest, then argues against it. Concede what is right about it; show why your reading still holds.

source: Mill, On Liberty, Chapter 2: he who knows only his own side
article: Some readers might say that The Great Gatsby celebrates the American Dream, but this essay argues that it critiques the Dream. Throughout the novel, Fitzgerald shows the corruption beneath the surface of wealth.

critiques.text_quote: Some readers might say that The Great Gatsby celebrates the American Dream, but this essay argues that it critiques the Dream.
critiques.question: You raise the celebratory reading in one clause and walk away from it. They Say / I Say: when you plant a naysayer, you have to do something with them. The strongest version is not 'celebrates the dream' but 'shows the dream's seductive power even as it shows the cost.' That reading is harder to dismiss.
critiques.why_it_matters: A counter named and abandoned signals to the reader you would rather not deal with it.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add a paragraph that takes the strongest version of the celebratory reading (the seduction is the point), then argue against it specifically. Quote the green-light passages on both sides of the question.

source: Booth, The Craft of Argument: the principle of charity
article: Defenders of the New Deal sometimes claim it helped people during hard times. This essay shows that it actually did the opposite.

critiques.text_quote: Defenders of the New Deal sometimes claim it helped people during hard times.
critiques.question: No serious defender of the New Deal argues from 'it helped people during hard times' as the load-bearing claim. The strongest defenses (Brinkley, Leuchtenburg, Katznelson) make institutional arguments: the New Deal stabilised the banking system, built the regulatory state, and reorganised the political coalitions. Booth's principle of charity: argue against the strongest reading.
critiques.why_it_matters: Defeating the cartoon defense leaves every serious one untouched.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace this counter with one of the institutional defenses (Brinkley, Leuchtenburg, or Katznelson on the racial structure of the welfare state), then argue against that.

source: They Say / I Say, Chapter 6: avoid the empty rebuttal
article: While some might disagree, the evidence is clear that the Glass-Steagall repeal in 1999 was the direct cause of the 2008 financial crisis.

critiques.text_quote: While some might disagree, the evidence is clear that
critiques.question: 'While some might disagree' followed by 'the evidence is clear' asserts a rebuttal without arguing one. The strongest critics of this causal story (Calomiris, Admati, the Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission's own dissent) argue that securitisation, capital requirements, and shadow banking were the mechanisms; Glass-Steagall is at most one factor. Your sentence does not engage them.
critiques.why_it_matters: Asserting that the evidence is clear is not the same as showing it is.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Either cite which evidence settles the question and acknowledge the strongest competing accounts (FCIC majority vs. dissent, for example), or weaken the verb to 'contributed to' and treat the causation as a live question.

source: Standard counter-argument diagnostic: wrong counter engaged
article: Some critics of Beloved argue that it is too violent for the high school classroom. This paper defends the novel by showing that its violence is purposeful and earned.

critiques.text_quote: Some critics of Beloved argue that it is too violent for the high school classroom.
critiques.question: The 'too violent for high school' debate is not the strongest critique of Beloved. The serious critical counter (set out by Stanley Crouch in The New Republic and discussed in the academy ever since) is whether the novel's mythic framing risks sentimentalising the historical record. That is the harder objection.
critiques.why_it_matters: Beating the easier counter while the harder one sits visible on the page weakens the defense.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Either engage the Crouch line directly (mythic framing vs. historical fidelity), or argue specifically that the curricular debate is a proxy for the deeper question and treat both.

source: Standard writing-center diagnostic: concession without integration
article: Admittedly, the New Deal did not end the Great Depression; wartime spending did. Nevertheless, the New Deal saved American capitalism.

critiques.text_quote: Admittedly, the New Deal did not end the Great Depression; wartime spending did. Nevertheless, the New Deal saved American capitalism.
critiques.question: You concede the most damaging point (the New Deal did not end the Depression) and then proceed as if the concession had no consequences. If wartime spending did the heavy lifting, what work is the 'saved capitalism' thesis still doing? The concession needs to reshape the claim, not just sit beside it.
critiques.why_it_matters: A concession that is not integrated reads as a checkbox, not an argument.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Either narrow the thesis to what the concession leaves intact ('the New Deal stabilised the financial system and the political coalition that won the war'), or take the concession seriously and abandon the broader claim.

article: Feminist critics argue that the novel reinforces patriarchal norms.

critiques.text_quote: Feminist critics argue that the novel reinforces patriarchal norms.
critiques.issue_label: false unity in the opposing camp
critiques.question: 'Feminist critics' is not one position. Gilbert and Gubar, bell hooks, and Judith Butler would read this novel in three different ways, and at least one of them would side with you. Flattening the camp into one voice hides the disagreements you could be using.
critiques.why_it_matters: Treating a divided opposing camp as a monolith makes the rebuttal too easy and the engagement too shallow.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Name the specific critic or tradition (e.g. 'second-wave readings since Gilbert and Gubar') and identify a counter-reading inside the same broad tradition that supports your claim.
critiques.replacement: Second-wave readings since Gilbert and Gubar argue that the novel reinforces patriarchal norms.

article: Every immigrant group in American history has eventually been accepted into the mainstream.

critiques.text_quote: Every immigrant group in American history has eventually been accepted into the mainstream.
critiques.question: 'Every' is the widest scope the language allows. The obvious counter (the experience of Black descendants of forced migration, of Chinese exclusion from 1882 to 1943, of post-9/11 Muslim Americans) sits visible on the page and the essay does not address it.
critiques.why_it_matters: A universal claim with visible counterexamples will be the first thing a reader challenges.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Narrow the scope to what the evidence supports (e.g. 'most European immigrant groups by the third generation'), or add a paragraph that engages the cases the broader claim has to explain.
critiques.replacement: Most European immigrant groups have been accepted into the mainstream by the third generation.
